1: The LORD speaks to Moses — the divine commission begins the final chapter of Exodus, which moves from completion (Exodus 39) to consecration and indwelling (Exodus 40). The fact that YHWH must command the erection of the tabernacle — that it does not happen by human initiative — emphasizes that the divine dwelling with humanity is always God's initiative. John 3:16: 'God so loved the world that he gave his one and only Son' — the move toward humanity is always from God's side. The tabernacle is not humanity's reaching toward God but God's reaching toward humanity, commanded and executed at divine initiative.
2: 'On the first day of the first month, set up the tabernacle.' The new year, the first day — a new beginning. The command to erect the tabernacle on the first day of the new year echoes the first day of creation (Genesis 1:3-5) and anticipates the 'first day of the week' of the resurrection (Matthew 28:1). The tabernacle's establishment on the new year's first day is a re-creation event. The resurrection, which inaugurates the new creation, similarly happens on the first day — the new week, the new age, the new creation beginning.
3: 'Put the ark of the testimony inside and screen it with the inner veil.' The ark — the most sacred object — goes in first, behind the veil. The veil is the barrier that keeps the overwhelming divine presence from destroying the approaching worshipper. Matthew 27:51: 'At that moment the curtain of the temple was torn in two from top to bottom.' The veil erected in Exodus 40 is the veil torn at the crucifixion — the barrier that the cross removes. What Moses erects in obedience, Christ removes in sacrifice.
4: 'Bring in the table and arrange what belongs on it; bring in the lampstand and light its lamps.' The table (bread of the Presence) and the lampstand (light) are placed in the holy place. Jesus says in the same chapter in John where he claims to be the bread of life: 'I am the light of the world' (John 8:12, immediately after the bread-of-life discourse context of Exodus 6). The two furnishings of the holy place — table and lampstand, bread and light — are both fulfilled in the single person of Christ. The holy place anticipates the one in whom all its furnishings are embodied.
5: 'Set the golden incense altar before the ark of the testimony, and hang the entrance screen.' The incense altar stands between the holy place and the most holy place — between the area of priestly service and the divine presence. It is the locus of intercession, the fragrant smoke rising toward the divine. Hebrews 7:25: 'He always lives to intercede for them.' Christ as the living incense altar stands between the congregation (holy place) and the Father (most holy place), filling the space between with perpetual intercession.
6: 'Place the altar of burnt offering at the entrance to the tabernacle.' The bronze altar at the entrance — the first encounter, the unavoidable threshold of sacrifice — is the foundational structure of the covenant approach. Every worshipper who comes to YHWH comes first to the altar of burnt offering. There is no entrance into the tabernacle complex except through sacrifice. 'God presented Christ as a sacrifice of atonement' (Romans 3:25) — the bronze altar at the entrance is the type of the cross as the unavoidable first encounter with the living God. There is no path to the Father except through the Son's sacrifice (John 14:6).
7: 'Place the basin between the tent of meeting and the altar and fill it with water.' The bronze basin between the altar (sacrifice) and the tent entrance (approach) provides the washing that prepares the priests for holy-place service. The order — altar (atonement) → basin (washing) → tent entrance (approach) — maps onto the gospel order: the cross (atonement) → baptism (washing) → access to the Father (approach). Hebrews 10:22: 'let us draw near to God... having our hearts sprinkled to cleanse us from a guilty conscience and having our bodies washed with pure water.'
8: 'Set up the surrounding court and hang the screen at the court gate.' The outer court — the boundary of sacred space — provides the first enclosure that separates the holy precincts from the surrounding world. The church is the new outer court: a visible, bounded community that marks the distinction between those gathered around the sacrifice and those outside. Hebrews 13:12-13 calls believers to 'go to him outside the camp' — the church's location is paradoxically outside (the old camp) and inside (the new court), gathered around the cross that redefines the sacred center.
9: 'Take the anointing oil and anoint the tabernacle and everything in it; consecrate it and all its furnishings, and it will be holy.' The anointing of the entire structure with oil — every piece of furniture, every curtain, every tent peg — consecrates the whole. The Spirit's anointing of the new-covenant community is similarly comprehensive: 'You have an anointing from the Holy One, and all of you know the truth' (1 John 2:20). The total anointing of the tabernacle is the type of the total anointing of the new-covenant community in Christ.
10: 'Anoint the altar of burnt offering with all its utensils and consecrate it, and it will be most holy.' The bronze altar — the place of sacrifice — is itself anointed and becomes 'most holy.' Whatever touches it becomes holy (Exodus 29:37). The cross is the most holy object of the new covenant: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us' (Galatians 3:13) — the cross, though bearing the curse and shame of crucifixion, is the most sacred place in history, the locus where divine love and divine justice meet in the person of the Son.
11: 'Anoint the basin and its stand and consecrate them.' The basin of washing is also consecrated with oil — the washing that cleanses the priests for service is itself anointed. Baptism as the new-covenant washing is administered in the name of the Trinity — it is a Spirit-anointed cleansing, not merely a water ritual. The oil-anointed basin is the type of the Spirit-accompanied baptism: 'one Lord, one faith, one baptism' (Ephesians 4:5), the single washing that constitutes membership in the new-covenant community.
12: 'Bring Aaron and his sons to the entrance to the tent of meeting and wash them with water.' The washing at the threshold — before vestition, before anointing, before service. Hebrews 10:22: 'having our hearts sprinkled to cleanse us from a guilty conscience and having our bodies washed with pure water.' The order is consistent throughout: washing precedes approach. The cross provides the cleansing (blood); baptism enacts the cleansing (water); together they constitute the threshold-crossing that grants access to the divine presence.
13: 'Dress Aaron in the sacred garments, anoint him, and consecrate him so that he may serve me as priest.' The threefold act: vestition → anointing → consecration. The same three elements are present in Jesus's preparation for his ministry: baptism (washing/vestition of righteousness) → Spirit descent (anointing) → 'This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased' (consecration/blessing). Matthew 3:13-17 is the NT equivalent of Exodus 40:12-13 — the Son is prepared for his priestly ministry through the same three movements.
14: 'Bring his sons and clothe them with tunics.' The sons receive the priestly garments from the one who has already been anointed. Galatians 3:27: 'All of you who were baptized into Christ have clothed yourselves with Christ.' The church receives its priestly vestiture derivatively — from and through the already-anointed Son. The sons' tunics come from the father's ordination; the church's righteousness comes from the Son's righteousness. No believer earns or generates their own priestly clothing; it is received from the anointed one.
15: 'Anoint them as you anointed their father so they may serve me as priests. Their anointing will establish a lasting priesthood for all generations to come.' The derived anointing of the sons replicates the father's anointing. 1 John 2:20: 'You have an anointing from the Holy One' — the church's anointing is derived from Christ's anointing, replicating in the body what the head received. 2 Corinthians 1:21-22: 'God... anointed us, set his seal of ownership on us, and put his Spirit in our hearts as a deposit' — the Father anoints the church with the same Spirit with which he anointed the Son.
16: 'Moses did everything just as the LORD had commanded him.' The comprehensive obedience of Moses — a single sentence that summarizes his entire execution of the divine instructions. This single sentence is the human-obedience climax of the book's second half. Philippians 2:8: 'he humbled himself by becoming obedient to death — even death on a cross.' Christ's obedience exceeds even Moses's: Moses obeyed in building; Christ obeyed in dying. The comprehensive obedience of Moses ('everything') is the type of the comprehensive obedience of Christ ('to death').
17: 'On the first day of the first month of the second year, the tabernacle was set up.' The precision of the redemptive calendar: a specific day, a specific month, a specific year. Galatians 4:4: 'when the fullness of time had come, God sent his Son.' The tabernacle erected on the specific first day of the first month of the second year is the OT pattern of the incarnation that occurred 'at the right time' (Romans 5:6). Divine redemption happens in history, at specific moments — not in timeless myth but in dateable events.
18: Moses set up the tabernacle: bases, frames, crossbars, pillars — the structural elements assembled in order. Paul's description of the church's construction is similarly ordered: 'built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets, with Christ Jesus himself as the chief cornerstone. In him the whole building is joined together and rises to become a holy temple in the Lord' (Ephesians 2:20-21). The careful, ordered assembly of the tabernacle structure — each element in its proper place — is the type of the Spirit-ordered construction of the new-covenant temple.
19: 'He spread the tent covering over the tabernacle and laid the outer cover on top — just as the LORD had commanded Moses.' The covering goes over the structure — the inner beauty is hidden from the outside world. Isaiah 53:2: 'He had no beauty or majesty to attract us to him.' The tabernacle's beautiful interior (gold, fine linen, gemstones) is covered by the humble outer coverings of dyed leather. Christ's divine glory is similarly covered by the humble outer covering of human flesh — 'he made himself nothing, taking the very nature of a servant' (Philippians 2:7), though within is the fullness of the Godhead.
20: Moses placed the testimony tablets in the ark, inserted the carrying poles, and set the mercy seat on top. The sequence is theological: law first, then mercy seat on top. The tablets of the law go inside the ark; the mercy seat — the kapporet, the atoning cover — is placed over them. Paul's logic in Romans 3:25-26 follows this structure: Christ as the hilasterion (mercy seat) is placed by God 'to demonstrate his justice... so as to be just and the one who justifies.' Mercy sits on top of justice; the mercy seat covers the law — not canceling it but fulfilling and covering it through Christ's atonement.
21: 'He brought the ark into the tabernacle and hung the inner veil, screening off the ark of the testimony — just as the LORD had commanded Moses.' Moses hangs the veil that will separate the ark (divine presence) from the holy place (priestly service). The veil that Moses erects in obedience is the veil that the cross tears in fulfillment. Matthew 27:51: 'At that moment the curtain of the temple was torn in two from top to bottom.' The top-down tearing of the veil — not from human hands below but from divine hands above — signals that God himself has removed the barrier that he himself established.
22: Moses set the table on the north side outside the inner veil. The table with its bread of the Presence stands perpetually before the Lord, representing the covenant community in the divine presence. The Lord's Supper is the new-covenant table: 'This is my body, given for you' (Luke 22:19) — the bread on the table is no longer the twelve loaves representing the twelve tribes but the one bread of the one body of Christ. The community no longer brings bread to represent themselves; they receive bread that represents Christ, given for them.
23: 'He arranged the bread on the table before the LORD, just as the LORD had commanded Moses.' The bread of the Presence arranged in order — twelve loaves in two rows (Leviticus 24:5-6). The orderly presentation of the covenant people before the LORD. In Revelation 7:4-8 the new-covenant community is also presented in ordered array: 144,000 from the twelve tribes (symbolic completeness). The ordered bread of the Presence is the type of the complete, ordered new-covenant community presented before God.
24: Moses placed the lampstand in the holy place, opposite the table, on the south side. The lampstand on the south faces the table on the north — light illuminates the bread. John 6:35: 'I am the bread of life'; John 8:12: 'I am the light of the world.' The pairing of bread and light in the holy place mirrors the pairing of incarnation and revelation: Christ feeds and illuminates. The lampstand that lights the table is the type of the light that illuminates the feast of the new covenant — the Spirit who sheds abroad the knowledge of Christ (2 Corinthians 4:6).
25: 'He lit the lamps before the LORD, just as the LORD had commanded Moses.' The lampstand is lit — the light burns continually in the holy place. The lampstand that burns before the LORD is the type of the church's witness in the world: 'You are the light of the world... let your light shine before others' (Matthew 5:14-16). But the ultimate fulfillment is Christ himself: 'The city does not need the sun or the moon to shine on it, for the glory of God gives it light, and the Lamb is its lamp' (Revelation 21:23). The lamped holy place is the seed of the new Jerusalem that needs no sun.
26: Moses placed the golden incense altar in the holy place in front of the inner veil. The incense altar's position — between the congregation's approach and the divine inner presence, directly before the veil — is the position of the intercessor: between the people and God. Hebrews 9:24: 'Christ did not enter a sanctuary made with human hands... he entered heaven itself, now to appear for us in God's presence.' Christ in the heavenly holy place, before the Father, is the living incense altar — his presence before the Father is itself the intercession.
27: 'And burned aromatic incense on it, just as the LORD had commanded Moses.' The incense burns — the fragrant prayer ascends. Revelation 5:8: 'the golden bowls full of incense, which are the prayers of God's people.' The golden incense altar where Moses burns aromatic incense is the type of the heavenly altar where the prayers of the saints ascend, mixed with the incense of Christ's own intercession (Revelation 8:3-4). The earthly incense altar that Moses lights is the gateway to the heavenly altar that the risen Christ tends.
28: Moses hung the screen at the tabernacle's entrance. The entrance screen (not the inner veil, which screens the most holy place, but the outer screen) is the boundary between the holy place and the outer world. The church's gathering space is bounded by this outer screen — the threshold between the community of worship and the surrounding world. Matthew 18:20: 'where two or three gather in my name, there am I with them' — the gathered community with Christ present is the new outer screen, marking the space of his presence.
29: Moses placed the altar of burnt offering at the tabernacle's entrance and offered the first burnt offering and grain offering. The first sacrifice consecrates the altar and begins the covenant worship. Jesus's first public act at the Jordan — receiving baptism and the Spirit — similarly consecrates the beginning of his priestly ministry. But the 'first offering' of Christ's ministry that the bronze altar anticipates is the cross: 'he offered himself unblemished to God' (Hebrews 9:14) — the one sacrifice that all the daily burnt offerings pointed toward.
30: Moses placed the basin between the tent of meeting and the altar and filled it with water for washing. The basin of washing — between the sacrifice (altar) and the approach (tent entrance) — is the purification step between atonement and access. The gospel's order is preserved: the cross (altar, atonement) → baptism (basin, washing) → the church's gathered life (tent of meeting, access). None of the three steps can be bypassed; each flows from and leads to the next in the divinely ordered sequence of approach to God.
31: Moses, Aaron, and Aaron's sons washed their hands and feet at the basin. The hands and feet — the instruments of action and movement — are specifically washed. John 13:10: after Jesus washes the disciples' feet, he says, 'A person who has had a bath needs only to wash their feet; their whole body is clean.' The basin's hand-and-foot washing is the ongoing purification of one who has already been bathed in the initial washing (baptism): the daily walking in the world requires the ongoing foot-washing of repentance and forgiveness (1 John 1:9).
32: 'Whenever they entered the tent of meeting or approached the altar, they washed — just as the LORD had commanded Moses.' The washing is habitual — every entry, every approach. The regular, repeated washing is the OT form of the NT's ongoing cleansing: 'If we walk in the light, as he is in the light... the blood of Jesus, his Son, purifies us from all sin' (1 John 1:7). The ongoing purification as a habitual practice, not a one-time event, is the lifestyle of those who 'draw near to God' (Hebrews 7:25; Exodus 10:22) through Christ.
33: 'He set up the surrounding court around the tabernacle and altar and hung the screen at the court gate. So Moses completed all the work.' The completion formula: Moses 'completed all the work.' Genesis 2:2: 'By the seventh day God had finished the work.' Moses completing the tabernacle work is the second creation-completion in Scripture. Jesus's 'It is finished' (John 19:30) is the third — and the final. The sequence is: creation completed (Genesis 2:2) → tabernacle completed (Exodus 40:33) → atonement completed (John 19:30) → new creation completed (Revelation 21:6: 'It is done').
34: 'Then the cloud covered the tent of meeting, and the glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle.' The climax of Exodus — the divine glory filling the completed tabernacle. This is the purpose toward which every chapter of the book has moved: YHWH dwelling with his redeemed people in the completed structure. John 1:14: 'The Word became flesh and made his dwelling (eskenosen — pitched his tent/tabernacled) among us. We have seen his glory (doxa), the glory of the one and only Son.' The glory-cloud filling the tabernacle is the type; the incarnate Son is the antitype. In the Son, the divine glory takes up permanent, personal residence in the human.
35: 'Moses was unable to enter the tent of meeting, because the cloud had settled on it and the glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle.' The unapproachable, overpowering divine glory — even Moses, who had spoken with YHWH face to face (Exodus 33:11), cannot enter. Hebrews 10:19-22 announces the transformation: 'We have confidence to enter the most holy place by the blood of Jesus... let us draw near to God with a sincere heart and with the full assurance that faith brings.' What Moses could not enter, Christ has opened: the way into the presence of the unapproachable glory is now available to every believer through the blood of the Son.
36: 'Whenever the cloud lifted from above the tabernacle, the Israelites set out on the next stage of their journey.' The cloud as the guide of the covenant community's pilgrimage — the divine presence that directs movement. John 16:13: 'when he, the Spirit of truth, comes, he will guide you into all the truth.' The cloud-guide of the wilderness pilgrimage is the type of the Spirit-guide of the new-covenant community's pilgrimage. The community follows the divine presence: not self-directed but Spirit-led, not setting their own course but following where the cloud lifts.
37: 'But if the cloud did not lift, they did not set out — not until the day it lifted.' The community's movement is entirely determined by the divine initiative. Acts 16:6-10: the Spirit twice prevents Paul and his companions from going in a particular direction, then opens the Macedonian way. The apostolic mission moves by the Spirit's lifting and settling — the same cloud-guidance that directed Israel in the wilderness directs the church in its mission. The community waits on divine direction rather than acting on human initiative.
38: 'The cloud of the LORD rested on the tabernacle by day, and fire glowed in the cloud by night — visible to all the house of Israel throughout all their journeys.' The final verse of Exodus: the visible, continuous divine presence with the entire covenant community, day and night, throughout every stage of the journey. Revelation 21:3: 'God's dwelling place is now among the people, and he will dwell with them. They will be his people, and God himself will be with them and be their God.' The last verse of Exodus is the seed; the vision of Revelation 21-22 is the flower. The cloud-and-fire presence that accompanied Israel through the wilderness is the type of the final, permanent, unmediated divine dwelling with humanity that is the goal of all redemptive history. Emmanuel — God with us — is the beginning (Matthew 1:23), the middle (John 1:14), and the end (Revelation 21:3) of the gospel.
